lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- Negotiation Basics -- Finding Common Ground
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Natalie Viegas | Aula 13 -- Negotiation Basics

listenings.text: Hi Natalie, this is Daniel. I wanted to give you a quick briefing before tomorrow's negotiation with the government client. Here is what we know. Their main concern is the price, so expect a tough conversation on the bottom line. If they pushed too hard, we would lose our margin, so we cannot move below twelve percent. However, we do have some leverage: we are the only vendor that can deliver before the deadline. My advice is simple. Do not open with our final number. Start a little higher, listen for their priorities, and look for a trade-off. If they asked for a lower price, you could offer a longer contract in return. The goal is a win-win, not a winner. If we found common ground on the timeline, the price would feel reasonable to them. Stay calm, never make a concession without getting something back, and if the talks reached a deadlock, just say you need to check with the team. You are ready for this. Good luck.

Hi, this is Natalie, recording a quick note after the negotiation. It went really well. We reached a deal that works for both sides. At first the client focused only on the bottom line, and we almost hit a deadlock. But then I asked about their priorities, and I realized the timeline mattered more than the price. So I offered a trade-off: a small concession on cost in exchange for a longer contract. They accepted the terms, and we settled at a fair number. I kept reminding myself: if I lost my patience, I would lose the deal. In the end we found common ground, and it became a real win-win. I never had to walk away. I am proud of how calm I stayed, and I think this is the most valuable skill I have built so far. Next time I will feel even more confident at the table.

extra_audio.text: First, you prepare and decide your bottom line before the meeting. Then you open the conversation and listen for the other side's priorities. After that, you propose a trade-off instead of just lowering your price. Next, you make a small concession only if you get something back in return. Finally, you confirm the terms and settle on a deal that is a real win-win.
